planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in dry to moist, well-drained, rocky to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; blunt-lobed woodsia is the most drought-tolerant native eastern fern and an excellent choice for dry, rocky shaded slopes and walls.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container-grown plants in spring in dry to moist, rocky, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; blunt-lobed woodsia establishes readily and is among the most drought-tolerant of all native ferns, making it uniquely valuable for challenging dry, rocky, shaded garden situations. Woodsia obtusa is a delicate-looking but tough native fern widely distributed across eastern North America from Canada to the Gulf states, producing compact, 8–18 inch, slightly hairy, light green fronds that are twice-pinnate with distinctive blunt-tipped (obtuse) lobes from which it takes its name; unlike most ferns it thrives in well-drained, even dry, rocky, alkaline soil such as limestone ledges and rocky woods. An excellent candidate for rock gardens, shaded dry walls, and naturalized areas under trees where soil is shallow or competitive. Semi-evergreen in mild winters.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; European wood sedge is a graceful, arching, loosely clumping sedge that naturalizes beautifully in shaded woodland gardens and tolerates deep shade better than most sedges.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; European wood sedge tolerates deep shade remarkably well and makes an elegant, naturalizing ground layer under trees. Carex sylvatica is a fine-textured European sedge that grows in loose, arching clumps 12–24 inches tall with bright green, very narrow leaves that cascade gracefully and slender, nodding, green flower spikes in early summer; it naturalizes slowly by self-seeding under deciduous trees and makes a beautiful, fine-textured woodland ground cover that contrasts well with broad-leaved shade plants like hostas, hellebores, and astilbe. More tolerant of dry shade than many sedges once established, and adaptable to a range of soil types from heavy clay to sandy loam. Largely ignored by deer and rabbit.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, woodland soil for natural cold stratification and spring germination; forest phacelia is a true shade-tolerant annual-biennial wildflower that naturalizes readily under deciduous trees, blooming in spring before the canopy leafs out and filling gaps in the woodland floor with lavender-blue flowers beloved by native bees.

directions: Scatter seeds in fall in moist, woodland soil under deciduous trees or in shaded beds; seeds need cold stratification and will germinate in early spring when temperatures warm. Forest phacelia is a native annual or biennial wildflower of eastern North American forests (Appalachian region south to Alabama) that is one of the rare shade-tolerant native annuals — it germinates and blooms in the dappled light of early spring before the tree canopy fills in, producing ferny, deeply divided, slightly hairy leaves and clusters of 5-petaled lavender-blue flowers with prominent violet stamens that are outstanding for early-season native bees, especially bumble bee queens just emerging from dormancy. It self-seeds reliably in suitable shaded, moist sites to form a self-sustaining colony. Not widely available commercially but worth seeking out from native plant nurseries; grows easily from fresh seed collected in summer.
sources: Natural Areas Journal, Appalachian Wildflower Research

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in dry to moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, sandy to loamy soil in partial shade to full sun; yellow star grass blooms prolifically in spring and early summer and is one of the native wildflowers that tolerates the light shade and root competition under pine trees — a very useful quality for southern landscapes.

directions: Plant small corms 1–2 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic soil; yellow star grass tolerates both dry and moderately moist conditions and is easily established in open woodland, pine barrens, and prairie settings. Hypoxis hirsuta is a tiny, charming native wildflower found throughout eastern North America from the Great Plains to the Atlantic coast, producing grass-like, hairy leaves 4–8 inches long and star-shaped, six-petaled, brilliant yellow flowers 1/2–3/4 inch across held on slender stems above the foliage from April through June; despite its small stature, a colony in full bloom is eye-catching. It grows from a corm and tolerates a wide range of conditions from dry pine woods to moist meadows, making it an adaptable choice for naturalizing in rough grass, woodland edges, and pollinator gardens. Native bees visit the flowers readily for pollen.

planting.note: Plant corms immediately after division in late summer or early fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil; this southeastern native is distinct from the more northern E. americanum and is the appropriate trout lily species for Gulf Coast-adjacent woodland gardens in zones 6–9.

directions: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep immediately after division in late summer or early fall in moist, leafy, slightly acidic woodland soil under deciduous trees; do not let corms dry out before planting. Southern yellow trout lily (Erythronium rostratum) is the southeastern counterpart to the familiar E. americanum, native to the coastal plain and adjacent Piedmont from Kentucky south to Louisiana and east to Georgia, blooming in February through April with nodding yellow flowers with distinctly reflexed petals and a characteristic beaked (rostrate) capsule that distinguishes it from other yellow Erythronium species; the green leaves with mottled brown spots are the same beautiful trout-spotted appearance as other species. This is a spring ephemeral that goes fully dormant by late spring — pair with late-emerging hostas or ferns to cover the gap. A valuable species for regional provenance in Gulf South woodland gardens.
